extra.reasoning:
The property globally(opted_out implies not sent) holds on all reachable executions.

Key observations:
1. sent becomes true ONLY via t_send_allowed (on send_attempt with guard "not opted_out"). At the moment sent is set true, opted_out is false, so the implication is satisfied (antecedent false).
2. opted_out becomes true ONLY via t_optout, which in the SAME atomic update also sets sent := false. So immediately after opting out: opted_out=true, sent=false — property holds.
3. opted_out is "sticky/absorbing": no transition ever resets opted_out to false. t_optout sets it true; t_send_allowed, t_send_blocked, and t_tick never modify opted_out. Hence once opted_out=true it remains true.
4. While opted_out=true, the only events touching sent are send_attempt (fires t_send_blocked because the guard "if opted_out" matches → sent:=false) and tick (sets sent:=false). t_send_allowed cannot fire because its guard "not opted_out" is false. Therefore sent can never be set true once opted_out is true; it can only be (re)set false.

Combining: in every reachable state, either opted_out=false (implication vacuously true) or opted_out=true with sent necessarily false. No 16-event (or any-length) sequence can produce a state with opted_out=true and sent=true. The property is satisfied.
